ES-CARDINALITY-THRESHOLD.description: Values above 40000 consume a lot of memory without significantly improving precision.
ES-CARDINALITY-THRESHOLD.suggestion: Set precision_threshold between 1000 and 40000 for the best memory/accuracy balance.

ES-BUCKET-SCRIPT-PATH.title: bucket_script requires buckets_path to reference sibling aggregations
ES-BUCKET-SCRIPT-PATH.description: Without buckets_path, the bucket_script script has no access to sibling aggregation values.

ES-BUCKET-SEL-FIELDS.title: bucket_selector requires both buckets_path and script
ES-BUCKET-SEL-FIELDS.description: bucket_selector needs both fields: references to sibling aggregations and the filter condition.
ES-BUCKET-SEL-FIELDS.suggestion: Add buckets_path (references) and script (condition) to bucket_selector.

ES-MOVING-AVG-DEPRECATED.title: moving_avg is deprecated — use moving_fn instead
ES-MOVING-AVG-DEPRECATED.description: The moving_avg aggregation was replaced by moving_fn in recent versions of Elasticsearch.
ES-MOVING-AVG-DEPRECATED.suggestion: Replace moving_avg with moving_fn using MovingFunctions.unweightedAvg(values).

ES-AGG-DEPTH.title: Deeply nested aggregations (> 4 levels) impact memory and performance
ES-AGG-DEPTH.description: Aggregations with more than 4 nesting levels impact cluster memory and performance.
ES-AGG-DEPTH.suggestion: Restructure aggregations to reduce nesting or use composite aggregation.

ES-FUNC-SCORE-QUERY.title: function_score without query evaluates ALL documents
ES-FUNC-SCORE-QUERY.description: Without an inner query, function_score applies scoring functions to all documents in the index.
ES-FUNC-SCORE-QUERY.suggestion: Add a query inside function_score to filter documents before applying functions.

ES-FUNC-SCORE-REPLACE.title: boost_mode 'replace' ignores the original relevance score
ES-FUNC-SCORE-REPLACE.description: With boost_mode: replace, the original query score is completely discarded.
ES-FUNC-SCORE-REPLACE.suggestion: Use boost_mode: 'multiply' or 'sum' to combine the original score with the function score.

ES-SCRIPT-SCORE-LANG.title: script_score without lang defaults to 'painless'
ES-SCRIPT-SCORE-LANG.description: Without specifying the language, Elasticsearch assumes painless. It is good practice to be explicit.
ES-SCRIPT-SCORE-LANG.suggestion: Add lang: 'painless' to make the intent clear.

ES-CONST-SCORE-BOOST.title: constant_score without boost defaults to 1.0
ES-CONST-SCORE-BOOST.description: Without specifying boost, all documents receive a score of 1.0, which may not be the intent.
ES-CONST-SCORE-BOOST.suggestion: Add boost: 1.5 (or desired value) to make the score explicit.

ES-GEO-DIST-UNIT.title: geo_distance without unit defaults to meters
ES-GEO-DIST-UNIT.description: Without specifying the unit, the distance is interpreted in meters, which may be unexpected.
ES-GEO-DIST-UNIT.suggestion: Add unit: 'km', 'mi', 'm', etc. to make the intent explicit.

ES-GEO-BBOX-LAT.title: geo_bounding_box: top_left latitude must be greater than bottom_right latitude
ES-GEO-BBOX-LAT.description: The bounding box coordinates are inverted — top_left must have a greater latitude than bottom_right.
ES-GEO-BBOX-LAT.suggestion: Swap top_left and bottom_right coordinates, or correct the latitude values.

ES-GEO-SHAPE-REL.title: geo_shape without relation defaults to 'intersects'
ES-GEO-SHAPE-REL.description: Without specifying the spatial relation, Elasticsearch uses 'intersects', which may not be the intent.
ES-GEO-SHAPE-REL.suggestion: Add relation: 'intersects', 'within', or 'contains' to make the intent explicit.

ES-KNN-K.title: knn requires the 'k' field (number of nearest neighbors)
ES-KNN-K.description: Without 'k', the knn query does not know how many neighbors to return.
ES-KNN-K.suggestion: Add k: 10 (or your desired number) to the knn query.

ES-KNN-CANDIDATES.title: knn without num_candidates may reduce recall
ES-KNN-CANDIDATES.description: Without num_candidates, Elasticsearch uses a default value that may not be sufficient for good accuracy.
ES-KNN-CANDIDATES.suggestion: Set num_candidates to at least 1.5 * k for good accuracy (e.g. k=10 → num_candidates=15).

MGO-SV-001.title: Required field missing for your collection rules
MGO-SV-001.description: The document you insert is missing a field your team marked as mandatory in the validation rules.
MGO-SV-001.suggestion: Add the missing field with a sensible value, or update the rules if the requirement changed.
MGO-SV-001.studentExplanation: Think of a form with a required box left empty: the office will reject it. Your insert is that form and one required box is empty.
MGO-SV-001.voiceSummary: Insert is missing a field your rules say must be present. Add it or relax the rules.
MGO-SV-001.technicalMessage: MGO-SV-001: insert document omits a key listed in $jsonSchema required; violates collection validator.

MGO-SV-002.title: Extra field not allowed by your rules
MGO-SV-002.description: The document includes a field that your validation rules do not allow.
MGO-SV-002.suggestion: Remove the unexpected field or extend the rules to list it under allowed properties.
MGO-SV-002.studentExplanation: Like bringing an extra item to a potluck when the host said only three dishes—your data brought something not on the approved list.
MGO-SV-002.voiceSummary: A field is present that your schema forbids. Drop it or allow it in the schema.
MGO-SV-002.technicalMessage: MGO-SV-002: additionalProperties:false rejects a key present on the insert payload.

MGO-SV-003.title: Value type does not match the rule
MGO-SV-003.description: A field’s value looks like the wrong kind of data compared to what your rules expect.
MGO-SV-003.suggestion: Match the value to the expected shape (text, number, date, list, etc.) or adjust the rule if the model changed.
MGO-SV-003.studentExplanation: It is like writing a word where a number was asked—the slot shape does not match what you put in.
MGO-SV-003.voiceSummary: A field’s value type does not match the schema. Fix the value or the schema.
MGO-SV-003.technicalMessage: MGO-SV-003: insert value JSON kind incompatible with $jsonSchema property type union for that field.

LUA-001.title: Fragile error handling in the script
LUA-001.description: The script calls Redis in a way that stops the whole script when a single command fails.
LUA-001.suggestion: Use the safer call style so failures can be handled without aborting the entire script.
LUA-001.studentExplanation: Like pulling one wrong lever that shuts down the whole factory line instead of flashing a warning you can fix.
LUA-001.voiceSummary: Prefer the safer Redis call so one command error does not kill the whole script.
LUA-001.technicalMessage: LUA-001: redis.call() used; redis.pcall() recommended for error isolation in Redis Lua.

LUA-002.title: Loop may never finish
LUA-002.description: A loop looks endless because nothing obvious stops it.
LUA-002.suggestion: Add a clear stop condition, a counter limit, or an exit path so the script cannot spin forever.
LUA-002.studentExplanation: Like a merry-go-round with no brake—if nothing changes, it could spin without stopping.
LUA-002.voiceSummary: Possible infinite loop detected. Add a finite exit or break.
LUA-002.technicalMessage: LUA-002: while/repeat construct has no verifiable break/exit (infinite loop heuristic).

LUA-003.title: Global variable in the script
LUA-003.description: The script writes to a global name instead of a local one, which is slower and easier to misuse.
LUA-003.suggestion: Declare variables with local so each run stays tidy and faster.
LUA-003.studentExplanation: Like writing everyone’s notes on a shared whiteboard instead of your own notepad—others might overwrite and it is messier.
LUA-003.voiceSummary: Use local variables instead of globals in Lua for clarity and speed.
LUA-003.technicalMessage: LUA-003: assignment to global (non-local) in Redis Lua; prefer local bindings.

LUA-004.title: Keys and script may disagree in cluster mode
LUA-004.description: The script may touch keys that were not declared up front, which breaks in clustered setups.
LUA-004.suggestion: Pass every key you read or write through the declared keys list and use KEYS[n] inside the script.
LUA-004.studentExplanation: Like a delivery list that names three doors but the driver tries a fourth—cluster mode needs the exact named doors.
LUA-004.voiceSummary: Declare all keys in KEYS and match numkeys for cluster-safe Lua.
LUA-004.technicalMessage: LUA-004: KEYS[] usage vs numkeys / cross-slot key access heuristic in LuaAnalyzer.

LUA-005.title: Arguments treated as numbers without converting
LUA-005.description: Script arguments from the client are text; using them in math without converting can misbehave.
LUA-005.suggestion: Convert arguments with a numeric cast before doing arithmetic.
LUA-005.studentExplanation: Like doing math on price tags still in their plastic sleeves—the number is there but you should unwrap it first.
LUA-005.voiceSummary: Wrap ARGV values with tonumber before arithmetic.
LUA-005.technicalMessage: LUA-005: ARGV[] used in arithmetic without tonumber() wrapper.

LUA-006.title: Very long script
LUA-006.description: The script is long enough that it is hard to read, test, and reuse safely.
LUA-006.suggestion: Split logic into smaller scripts or load helpers once and reuse their hash.
LUA-006.studentExplanation: Like a single recipe that fills ten pages—easier to follow when split into steps or cards.
LUA-006.voiceSummary: Script exceeds fifty lines. Consider splitting or shared modules.
LUA-006.technicalMessage: LUA-006: Lua script line count > 50; maintainability / SCRIPT LOAD recommendation.

LUA-007.title: Many Redis calls inside a loop
LUA-007.description: Each loop trip talks to the server again, which can be slow and noisy under load.
LUA-007.suggestion: Batch work with a client-side pipeline outside Lua or shrink what each iteration does.
LUA-007.studentExplanation: Like calling the front desk once per sock instead of asking for the whole drawer—many small trips add up.
LUA-007.voiceSummary: redis.call inside a loop without client pipelining. Batch outside or reduce iterations.
LUA-007.technicalMessage: LUA-007: redis.call inside loop body; N round-trips per execution (performance warning).

RDS-PIP-001.title: Risky command queued inside a transaction block
RDS-PIP-001.description: A command that can freeze or wipe data was placed inside a queued transaction.
RDS-PIP-001.suggestion: Move broad scans and full-database wipes outside MULTI/EXEC or avoid them entirely in production.
RDS-PIP-001.studentExplanation: Like locking the building doors and then planning to search every room with a bulldozer—dangerous while the queue is active.
RDS-PIP-001.voiceSummary: KEYS or FLUSH inside MULTI/EXEC. Remove or relocate.
RDS-PIP-001.technicalMessage: RDS-PIP-001: KEYS * or FLUSH* detected inside MULTI/EXEC pipeline.

RDS-PIP-002.title: Very large batch of queued commands
RDS-PIP-002.description: More than a thousand commands are queued in one go, which can time out or spike memory.
RDS-PIP-002.suggestion: Split into smaller batches or raise client timeouts only after measuring safe sizes.
RDS-PIP-002.studentExplanation: Like stacking a thousand plates for one trip—easier and safer in several smaller stacks.
RDS-PIP-002.voiceSummary: Over one thousand pipelined or queued commands. Split batches.
RDS-PIP-002.technicalMessage: RDS-PIP-002: pipeline or MULTI queue > 1000 commands (RedisAnalyzer).

RDS-PIP-003.title: Watch without a matching finish
RDS-PIP-003.description: The script starts watching keys for changes but never completes the intended transaction.
RDS-PIP-003.suggestion: Pair watch with MULTI, queued work, EXEC, or explicitly cancel with UNWATCH.
RDS-PIP-003.studentExplanation: Like setting an alarm but never pressing start or stop—the system stays waiting awkwardly.
RDS-PIP-003.voiceSummary: WATCH with no later EXEC in script. Finish or UNWATCH.
RDS-PIP-003.technicalMessage: RDS-PIP-003: WATCH without matching EXEC in same script (cross-script heuristic).

RDS-ACL-001.title: Account rules look too powerful
RDS-ACL-001.description: The access-control line grants very wide command or key patterns.
RDS-ACL-001.suggestion: Narrow categories and key patterns to what that identity truly needs.
RDS-ACL-001.studentExplanation: Like giving a temp badge that opens every door including the vault—trim access to real needs.
RDS-ACL-001.voiceSummary: ACL user may be overprivileged. Tighten categories and key patterns.
RDS-ACL-001.technicalMessage: RDS-ACL-001: ACL SETUSER pattern suggests +@all or dangerous category breadth.

RDS-ACL-002.title: Read-only style rules with broad key reach
RDS-ACL-002.description: The profile is read-focused but still matches a very wide set of keys.
RDS-ACL-002.suggestion: Tighten the key pattern to real prefixes your app uses.
RDS-ACL-002.studentExplanation: Like a library pass that says reading only but works for every shelf in every branch—still risky if lost.
RDS-ACL-002.voiceSummary: Read-style ACL with very broad tilde pattern. Narrow prefixes.
RDS-ACL-002.technicalMessage: RDS-ACL-002: read-oriented ACL still uses overly broad ~* style glob.

RDS-ACL-003.title: Removing a protected default user
RDS-ACL-003.description: Deleting the default user can lock administration if no other superuser exists.
RDS-ACL-003.suggestion: Ensure another admin account exists before removing defaults.
RDS-ACL-003.studentExplanation: Like removing the only master key copy—make sure someone else can still open the office.
RDS-ACL-003.voiceSummary: ACL DELUSER on default user. Confirm another admin exists.
RDS-ACL-003.technicalMessage: RDS-ACL-003: ACL DELUSER default — operational lockout risk.

RDS-MOD-001.title: JSON module dependency
RDS-MOD-001.description: The command needs the RedisJSON module loaded on the server.
RDS-MOD-001.suggestion: Install and enable RedisJSON on every node before relying on this in production.
RDS-MOD-001.studentExplanation: Like needing a special attachment for your drill—without the module the command will not exist on plain Redis.
RDS-MOD-001.voiceSummary: JSON command requires RedisJSON module on the cluster.
RDS-MOD-001.technicalMessage: RDS-MOD-001: JSON.* command family (RedisJSON module required).

RDS-MOD-002.title: Search module dependency
RDS-MOD-002.description: The command needs RediSearch (or compatible) loaded.
RDS-MOD-002.suggestion: Provision the search module and index before using FT.* in production paths.
RDS-MOD-002.studentExplanation: Like expecting a bookstore search desk in a warehouse that only stacks boxes—add the search service first.
RDS-MOD-002.voiceSummary: FT search command needs RediSearch module deployed.
RDS-MOD-002.technicalMessage: RDS-MOD-002: FT.* command (RediSearch / secondary module dependency).

CAS-BATCH-001.title: Batch is extremely large
CAS-BATCH-001.description: The batch bundles many statements, which strains the coordinator.
CAS-BATCH-001.suggestion: Split into smaller batches or use asynchronous writes with a concurrency cap.
CAS-BATCH-001.studentExplanation: Like handing a courier fifty heavy boxes at once—split trips so nothing collapses.
CAS-BATCH-001.voiceSummary: Cassandra batch over fifty statements. Shrink or async writes.
CAS-BATCH-001.technicalMessage: CAS-BATCH-001: estimated batch statement count > 50 (coordinator pressure / ERROR severity in CqlAnalyzer).

CAS-BATCH-002.title: Batch crosses partition boundaries
CAS-BATCH-002.description: Statements target different partition keys, so you lose single-partition benefits.
CAS-BATCH-002.suggestion: Group writes that share one partition or send separate batches per partition.
CAS-BATCH-002.studentExplanation: Like mailing letters to many cities in one envelope—sort by destination before posting.
CAS-BATCH-002.voiceSummary: Batch spans multiple partitions. Prefer single-partition batches.

CAS-BATCH-003.title: Good use of an unlogged single-partition batch
CAS-BATCH-003.description: An unlogged batch targets one partition, which matches Cassandra’s happy path for throughput.
CAS-BATCH-003.suggestion: Keep this pattern; avoid mixing unrelated partitions in the same batch.
CAS-BATCH-003.studentExplanation: Like loading one moving truck for one neighborhood—efficient when everything shares the same stop.
CAS-BATCH-003.voiceSummary: Unlogged batch on one partition looks healthy.
CAS-BATCH-003.technicalMessage: CAS-BATCH-003: BEGIN UNLOGGED BATCH with single known partition (INFO advisory).

CAS-BATCH-004.title: Logged batch used where unlogged would do
CAS-BATCH-004.description: A durable cluster-wide batch wraps only one partition’s writes, which costs more than needed.
CAS-BATCH-004.suggestion: Switch to BEGIN UNLOGGED BATCH when every mutation shares the same partition key.
CAS-BATCH-004.studentExplanation: Like insured overnight shipping for a letter you could walk next door—lighter option fits.
CAS-BATCH-004.voiceSummary: Logged batch on single partition. Prefer UNLOGGED.
CAS-BATCH-004.technicalMessage: CAS-BATCH-004: LOGGED BATCH with single-partition DML — prefer UNLOGGED (CqlAnalyzer).

CAS-BATCH-005.title: Counter batch mixes regular writes
CAS-BATCH-005.description: A counter-only batch contains statements that are not counter updates.
CAS-BATCH-005.suggestion: Move inserts, deletes, and normal updates outside the counter batch.
CAS-BATCH-005.studentExplanation: Like a coins-only lane with someone trying to pay with a couch—split incompatible items.
CAS-BATCH-005.voiceSummary: COUNTER BATCH contains non-counter statements. Move them out.
CAS-BATCH-005.technicalMessage: CAS-BATCH-005: COUNTER BATCH contains non-counter DML (CqlAnalyzer.batchContainsNonCounterStatement).

CAS-LWT-001.title: Conditional write on a hot partition
CAS-LWT-001.description: Lightweight transactions on a busy partition key contend heavily and slow everyone.
CAS-LWT-001.suggestion: Spread hot keys, reduce Paxos use, or redesign access so consensus hits cooler partitions.
CAS-LWT-001.studentExplanation: Like asking everyone in a stadium line to re-vote each step—works but jams if the line is huge.
CAS-LWT-001.voiceSummary: LWT on hot or high-cardinality partition. Expect slowness.
CAS-LWT-001.technicalMessage: CAS-LWT-001: LWT (IF / IF NOT EXISTS) on hot partition heuristic (Paxos path).

CAS-LWT-002.title: Several conditional writes in one batch
CAS-LWT-002.description: Multiple lightweight transactions in a single batch do not behave as one atomic Paxos round.
CAS-LWT-002.suggestion: Keep at most one conditional statement per batch or run them outside BATCH.
CAS-LWT-002.studentExplanation: Like several veto votes stacked in one envelope—Cassandra still counts them separately.
CAS-LWT-002.voiceSummary: Multiple LWT statements in one BATCH. Use one or split.

CAS-LWT-003.title: Remember to read the yes-or-no flag
CAS-LWT-003.description: A conditional insert, update, or delete may do nothing if the check fails.
CAS-LWT-003.suggestion: In application code read the applied or CAS result before assuming data changed.
CAS-LWT-003.studentExplanation: Like asking “did the door actually lock?” instead of walking away assuming it clicked.
CAS-LWT-003.voiceSummary: Check wasApplied after IF / IF NOT EXISTS writes.
CAS-LWT-003.technicalMessage: CAS-LWT-003: driver should verify ResultSet.wasApplied() / CAS outcome for LWT DML.

CAS-UDF-001.title: Function stability not declared
CAS-UDF-001.description: The user-defined function does not state whether its results are deterministic.
CAS-UDF-001.suggestion: Add the deterministic clause your Cassandra version supports, or keep the body free of randomness and side effects.
CAS-UDF-001.studentExplanation: Like a recipe that never says bake time—engines need a hint whether output is repeatable.
CAS-UDF-001.voiceSummary: Cassandra UDF missing DETERMINISTIC declaration. Add or document purity.
CAS-UDF-001.technicalMessage: CAS-UDF-001: CREATE FUNCTION without DETERMINISTIC keyword (CqlAnalyzer.analyzeCreateFunction).

CAS-UDF-002.title: Function may run too long inside queries
CAS-UDF-002.description: The function body has heavy loops that can block reads and writes.
CAS-UDF-002.suggestion: Simplify logic, lower iteration counts, or move heavy work to the application tier.
CAS-UDF-002.studentExplanation: Like doing a marathon inside a checkout line—everyone behind you waits.
CAS-UDF-002.voiceSummary: UDF loop complexity high. Simplify or move work off node.
CAS-UDF-002.technicalMessage: CAS-UDF-002: CassandraJsParser loopRules — many loops or large numeric bound in UDF body.

CAS-UDF-003.title: Function touches the outside world
CAS-UDF-003.description: The function body appears to call network, disk, or other impure APIs.
CAS-UDF-003.suggestion: Remove external access; UDFs must stay pure and fast for safe execution in Cassandra.
CAS-UDF-003.studentExplanation: Like asking the bank teller to step out mid-transaction—breaks the safe sandbox.
CAS-UDF-003.voiceSummary: UDF shows I/O or impure API patterns. Remove them.
CAS-UDF-003.technicalMessage: CAS-UDF-003: CassandraJsParser impure() — JS/Java UDF body matched forbidden I/O patterns.

CAS-UDA-001.title: Aggregate missing starting value
CAS-UDA-001.description: Creating an aggregate without an initial state leaves behavior unclear.
CAS-UDA-001.suggestion: Provide INITCOND with a literal compatible with the state type, even if null.
CAS-UDA-001.studentExplanation: Like a scoreboard with no starting score—set zero or an agreed baseline.
CAS-UDA-001.voiceSummary: CREATE AGGREGATE lacks INITCOND. Add explicit initial state.
CAS-UDA-001.technicalMessage: CAS-UDA-001: CREATE AGGREGATE without INITCOND clause (CqlAnalyzer.analyzeCreateAggregate).

CAS-UDA-002.title: State function not found in script
CAS-UDA-002.description: The aggregate references a state function that this script never defines.
CAS-UDA-002.suggestion: Define SFUNC earlier in the migration or fix the name and keyspace.
CAS-UDA-002.studentExplanation: Like referencing chapter five in a booklet that only has four chapters.
CAS-UDA-002.voiceSummary: SFUNC name missing from script context. Define or correct it.
CAS-UDA-002.technicalMessage: CAS-UDA-002: SFUNC qualified name not present in SchemaRegistry (CqlAnalyzer).

CAS-TRG-001.title: Trigger class cannot be verified here
CAS-TRG-001.description: Triggers load Java classes at runtime; static analysis cannot see the classpath.
CAS-TRG-001.suggestion: Ship the JAR to every node and validate trigger behavior in staging.
CAS-TRG-001.studentExplanation: Like hiring a magician without a rehearsal—you must test on the real stage.
CAS-TRG-001.voiceSummary: Trigger Java class not statically verified. Test in staging.
CAS-TRG-001.technicalMessage: CAS-TRG-001: CREATE TRIGGER — Java class not on analyzer classpath (CqlAnalyzer.analyzeCreateTrigger).

CAS-TRG-002.title: Trigger targets system data
CAS-TRG-002.description: A trigger is attached to a system keyspace table, which Cassandra rejects.
CAS-TRG-002.suggestion: Point triggers only at application tables under your keyspaces.
CAS-TRG-002.studentExplanation: Like wiring a home alarm to the city hall breaker—off limits.
CAS-TRG-002.voiceSummary: Trigger on system table invalid. Use application tables.
CAS-TRG-002.technicalMessage: CAS-TRG-002: trigger ON system keyspace table (isSystemKeyspaceTable).

CAS-DCL-001.title: Role can log in but has no password
CAS-DCL-001.description: A login-capable role was created without a password clause.
CAS-DCL-001.suggestion: Add a strong password and explicitly set LOGIN or SUPERUSER only when needed.
CAS-DCL-001.studentExplanation: Like issuing an ID badge that opens doors but never sets a PIN—unsafe.
CAS-DCL-001.voiceSummary: CREATE ROLE without PASSWORD for login role. Add credentials.
CAS-DCL-001.technicalMessage: CAS-DCL-001: CREATE ROLE missing WITH PASSWORD when login-capable (RoleWithScan).

CAS-DCL-002.title: All-powerful grant or revoke
CAS-DCL-002.description: Granting or revoking every permission at once is unusually broad or risky.
CAS-DCL-002.suggestion: List specific permissions on specific resources instead of ALL.
CAS-DCL-002.studentExplanation: Like handing master keys to every room when one closet was requested.
CAS-DCL-002.voiceSummary: GRANT or REVOKE ALL style clause. Prefer least privilege.
CAS-DCL-002.technicalMessage: CAS-DCL-002: GRANT/REVOKE ALL or ALL PERMISSIONS pattern (CqlAnalyzer DCL).

CAS-DCL-003.title: Superuser flag enabled
CAS-DCL-003.description: The role is marked as superuser, which bypasses normal limits.
CAS-DCL-003.suggestion: Reserve superuser for break-glass accounts and use narrow roles elsewhere.
CAS-DCL-003.studentExplanation: Like giving airport security master override to every intern—keep it rare.
CAS-DCL-003.voiceSummary: SUPERUSER true on role. Use sparingly.
CAS-DCL-003.technicalMessage: CAS-DCL-003: SUPERUSER = true in CREATE/ALTER ROLE (CqlAnalyzer).

CAS-DCL-004.title: Keyspace-wide permission change
CAS-DCL-004.description: The grant or revoke applies to an entire keyspace, affecting every table inside.
CAS-DCL-004.suggestion: If only one table needs access, scope the statement to that table.
CAS-DCL-004.studentExplanation: Like painting every room because one wall needed touch-up—narrow the brush.
CAS-DCL-004.voiceSummary: GRANT or REVOKE on whole keyspace. Consider table scope.
CAS-DCL-004.technicalMessage: CAS-DCL-004: GRANT/REVOKE on keyspace resource (all tables).

CAS-DCL-005.title: Odd role nesting
CAS-DCL-005.description: A login-capable identity receives a role that cannot log in on its own, which is usually accidental.
CAS-DCL-005.suggestion: Enable LOGIN on the delegated role or grant a role meant for interactive use.
CAS-DCL-005.studentExplanation: Like giving your house key to a guest who only has a library card—mismatch of access types.
CAS-DCL-005.voiceSummary: Non-LOGIN role granted to LOGIN user. Review design.

CAS-AUTH-001.title: Password looks weak
CAS-AUTH-001.description: The password is short or only letters and digits.
CAS-AUTH-001.suggestion: Use at least eight characters mixing letters, numbers, and symbols for human accounts.
CAS-AUTH-001.studentExplanation: Like a bike lock with three digits—easy for others to guess.
CAS-AUTH-001.voiceSummary: Weak Cassandra password heuristic. Strengthen it.
CAS-AUTH-001.technicalMessage: CAS-AUTH-001: isWeakCassandraPassword — length < 8 or letters+digits only.

DDB-TX-001.title: Too many actions in one transaction
DDB-TX-001.description: The request lists more than one hundred steps, which DynamoDB rejects.
DDB-TX-001.suggestion: Split into multiple transactions or reorder work so unrelated writes are separate.
DDB-TX-001.studentExplanation: Like stuffing a hundred envelopes through a mail slot that only accepts ten at a time.
DDB-TX-001.voiceSummary: TransactItems over one hundred. Split the transaction.

DDB-TX-002.title: Reads and writes mixed in one transaction API
DDB-TX-002.description: The same payload mixes Get with write actions, which the write transaction API disallows.
DDB-TX-002.suggestion: Use the read-only transaction API for Gets or drop Gets from this payload.
DDB-TX-002.studentExplanation: Like trying to checkout and inventory-count in one impossible single receipt line.
DDB-TX-002.voiceSummary: TransactItems mixes Get with writes. Separate APIs.
DDB-TX-002.technicalMessage: DDB-TX-002: anyGet && anyWrite in same TransactItems (TransactWriteItems incompatibility).

DDB-TX-003.title: Condition check missing its rule text
DDB-TX-003.description: A condition check item has no expression to evaluate.
DDB-TX-003.suggestion: Add ConditionExpression describing what must be true before the check succeeds.
DDB-TX-003.studentExplanation: Like a referee asked to blow the whistle but no rule was written down.
DDB-TX-003.technicalMessage: DDB-TX-003: ConditionCheck item with empty ConditionExpression.

DDB-TX-004.title: Many tables touched in one transaction
DDB-TX-004.description: The transaction references more than one table, which is allowed but harder and costlier.
DDB-TX-004.suggestion: Document why it is intentional or split work; you can mark the payload as justified in tooling.
DDB-TX-004.studentExplanation: Like one contract signing for apartments in three cities—possible but needs a reason on file.
DDB-TX-004.voiceSummary: Multi-table transaction advisory. Confirm intent or split.
DDB-TX-004.technicalMessage: DDB-TX-004: distinct table count > 1 without multiTableJustified flag.

DDB-COND-001.title: Update helpers used inside a condition
DDB-COND-001.description: Functions meant for updates appear inside a condition expression, which DynamoDB does not support.
DDB-COND-001.suggestion: Move those checks into UpdateExpression or use a transaction item designed for checks.
DDB-COND-001.studentExplanation: Like using a oven timer to unlock a door—the gadget belongs in the kitchen step, not the lock.
DDB-COND-001.voiceSummary: if_not_exists or list_append in ConditionExpression. Remove or relocate.
DDB-COND-001.technicalMessage: DDB-COND-001: if_not_exists / list_append detected in ConditionExpression (DynamoDbExpressionAnalyzer).

DDB-COND-002.title: Exists-check missing a real field path
DDB-COND-002.description: The exists function needs a field path, not a value placeholder.
DDB-COND-002.suggestion: Pass a name path such as #pk or status, not a :value token.
DDB-COND-002.studentExplanation: Like asking “does it exist?” without saying what “it” is.
DDB-COND-002.voiceSummary: attribute_exists argument must be an attribute path.
DDB-COND-002.technicalMessage: DDB-COND-002: attribute_exists arity or non-PathExpr argument.

DDB-COND-003.title: Size check on a number field
DDB-COND-003.description: Length-style checks do not apply to numeric attributes in DynamoDB.
DDB-COND-003.suggestion: Compare numbers directly or store lengths in a separate attribute if you need them.
DDB-COND-003.studentExplanation: Like measuring the weight of a sound—numbers need math, not length rulers.
DDB-COND-003.voiceSummary: size() on Number or Number Set invalid. Use numeric compares.
DDB-COND-003.technicalMessage: DDB-COND-003: size() on N/NS attribute types (unsupported).

DDB-STR-001.title: Change stream on but no item expiry rules
DDB-STR-001.description: Streams only keep history for about a day unless you also manage item lifetime separately.
DDB-STR-001.suggestion: Turn on table TTL if rows should disappear on their own, alongside streaming.
DDB-STR-001.studentExplanation: Like recording security video without deciding how long to keep tapes—set retention for the rows too.
DDB-STR-001.voiceSummary: Stream enabled without TTL hint. Consider TimeToLiveSpecification.

DDB-STR-002.title: Stream consumer without a dead-letter path
DDB-STR-002.description: Lambda reads the stream but has no dead-letter queue for poison records.
DDB-STR-002.suggestion: Add on-failure routing so bad events cannot loop forever.
DDB-STR-002.studentExplanation: Like a mailroom with no damaged-parcel shelf—problem letters need a side bin.
DDB-STR-002.voiceSummary: Lambda stream mapping lacks DLQ. Add OnFailure or DeadLetterConfig.
DDB-STR-002.technicalMessage: DDB-STR-002: EventSourceMapping on stream without DestinationConfig.OnFailure / DeadLetterConfig.

DDB-BAK-001.title: Production backups appear off
DDB-BAK-001.description: Continuous backups look disabled while the payload marks a production environment.
DDB-BAK-001.suggestion: Enable continuous backups and verify restore drills regularly.
DDB-BAK-001.studentExplanation: Like leaving the fire extinguisher pin in during opening night—turn safety on for prod.
DDB-BAK-001.voiceSummary: Production flag with continuous backups disabled. Enable them.
DDB-BAK-001.technicalMessage: DDB-BAK-001: production && ContinuousBackupsStatus DISABLED / continuousBackupsEnabled false.

DDB-BAK-002.title: Restore target name matches source
DDB-BAK-002.description: Restoring into the same table name as the backup source risks overwriting live data.
DDB-BAK-002.suggestion: Restore to a new table name first, then carefully swap traffic.
DDB-BAK-002.studentExplanation: Like photocopying a book onto the original pages—use a blank notebook first.
DDB-BAK-002.voiceSummary: RestoreTable target equals source table name. Use a new name.
DDB-BAK-002.technicalMessage: DDB-BAK-002: RestoreTableFromBackup TargetTableName equals source table from BackupArn.

DDB-BAK-003.title: Point-in-time recovery disabled
DDB-BAK-003.description: Finer restore windows are off, so you only have ordinary backup granularity.
DDB-BAK-003.suggestion: Enable PITR when the table matters and you need minute-level recovery.
DDB-BAK-003.studentExplanation: Like having yearly photos instead of daily ones—you recover less detail.
DDB-BAK-003.voiceSummary: PITR disabled. Consider enabling for important tables.
DDB-BAK-003.technicalMessage: DDB-BAK-003: PointInTimeRecoveryStatus DISABLED or spec disables PITR.

DDB-API-001.title: Star query on a large table
DDB-API-001.description: Selecting every attribute on a big table forces manual pagination and heavy reads.
DDB-API-001.suggestion: Page with continuation keys and project only needed attributes.
DDB-API-001.studentExplanation: Like emptying a warehouse through a single door—go in batches and carry only what you need.
DDB-API-001.voiceSummary: SELECT star on large hinted table. Paginate and project.
DDB-API-001.technicalMessage: DDB-API-001: PartiQL SELECT * with QWERYS_TABLE_SIZE_BYTES hint > 1MB threshold.

DDB-API-002.title: Update missing partition key filter
DDB-API-002.description: The update statement does not clearly pin the partition key with an equality.
DDB-API-002.suggestion: Add WHERE with partition key equality and sort key if the table defines one.
DDB-API-002.studentExplanation: Like updating an address book without saying which contact—name the row first.
DDB-API-002.voiceSummary: PartiQL UPDATE needs partition key equality in WHERE.
DDB-API-002.technicalMessage: DDB-API-002: UPDATE without WHERE or without equality on partition key (PartiQL analyzer).

PNL-001.title: Loop may never stop
PNL-001.description: A loop’s condition always looks true and nothing obvious exits early.
PNL-001.suggestion: Add a finite bound, change the condition each pass, or return or break inside the body.
PNL-001.studentExplanation: Like a treadmill stuck on max with no stop cord—give yourself a way off.
PNL-001.voiceSummary: Painless while or for may not exit. Add break or finite condition.
PNL-001.technicalMessage: PNL-001: non-escaping while / infinite for heuristic (PainlessAnalyzer).

PNL-002.title: Field name not in the known list
PNL-002.description: The script reads a document field that your provided schema does not list.
PNL-002.suggestion: Fix the spelling or refresh the mapping list you pass to the analyzer.
PNL-002.studentExplanation: Like asking for aisle twelve in a store that only has ten aisles.
PNL-002.voiceSummary: doc field missing from schema set. Correct name or mapping.
PNL-002.technicalMessage: PNL-002: doc['field'] literal not in ctx.schemaFields.

PNL-003.title: Changing stored data in the wrong script type
PNL-003.description: A query-time script tries to rewrite the document source, which is not allowed there.
PNL-003.suggestion: Move changes to an update or ingest pipeline script instead.
PNL-003.studentExplanation: Like trying to rewrite a library book during a public reading—borrow the checkout desk instead.
PNL-003.voiceSummary: ctx._source assignment in query script forbidden. Use update or ingest.

PNL-004.title: Script must hand back a value
PNL-004.description: This script context expects a returned result but none appears.
PNL-004.suggestion: End with an explicit return that hands back the score or value the caller needs.
PNL-004.studentExplanation: Like a math quiz that says “show your answer” and you only showed work.
PNL-004.voiceSummary: Missing return in context that requires one.

PNL-005.title: Heavy loop inside an aggregation script
PNL-005.description: The script walks collections inside a per-bucket path, which can scale badly.
PNL-005.suggestion: Prefer doc values, parameters, or precomputed metrics instead of big loops per bucket.
PNL-005.studentExplanation: Like counting beans in every jar while customers wait at each checkout.
PNL-005.voiceSummary: Possible O(n) loop in aggregation script. Simplify.
PNL-005.technicalMessage: PNL-005: looksLikeLinearPass inside aggsSubtree loop (METHOD_CALL heuristic).

PNL-006.title: Document read without safety net
PNL-006.description: Field access can throw if the field is missing or the wrong type, and there is no try block.
PNL-006.suggestion: Wrap doc access in try/catch or guard with safe defaults.
PNL-006.studentExplanation: Like opening a locker without checking the number—have a plan if it jams.
PNL-006.voiceSummary: doc access without try/catch. Add guards.
PNL-006.technicalMessage: PNL-006: doc[...] present, no try keyword (ScriptException heuristic).

PNL-007.title: Randomness inside scoring logic
PNL-007.description: Random numbers make scores change run to run, which breaks caching and tests.
PNL-007.suggestion: Use fixed weights or pass any randomness through params from outside.
PNL-007.studentExplanation: Like rolling dice to grade homework—results won’t match tomorrow.
PNL-007.voiceSummary: Math.random in Painless. Remove for reproducible scoring.

ES-IP-001.title: Pipeline lacks failure handling
ES-IP-001.description: If a processor fails, the pipeline has no top-level or per-step recovery path.
ES-IP-001.suggestion: Add on_failure at the pipeline or step level so errors surface clearly.
ES-IP-001.studentExplanation: Like a factory line with no red stop button when a machine jams.
ES-IP-001.voiceSummary: Ingest pipeline missing on_failure. Add handlers.
ES-IP-001.technicalMessage: ES-IP-001: ingest pipeline JSON without pipeline or processor on_failure arrays.

ES-IP-002.title: Very long processor chain
ES-IP-002.description: More than twenty processors are chained, which is hard to maintain and debug.
ES-IP-002.suggestion: Split into smaller pipelines and link them with a pipeline processor.
ES-IP-002.studentExplanation: Like a recipe with twenty chapters—split into courses people can follow.
ES-IP-002.voiceSummary: Over twenty ingest processors. Split pipeline.

ES-IP-003.title: Nested pipeline reference
ES-IP-003.description: This step calls another pipeline by name, which static checks cannot verify.
ES-IP-003.suggestion: Confirm the child pipeline exists in every cluster before production rollout.
ES-IP-003.studentExplanation: Like linking to another book’s chapter without checking the book is on the shelf.
ES-IP-003.voiceSummary: Nested pipeline processor. Verify referenced pipeline exists.
ES-IP-003.technicalMessage: ES-IP-003: processor type pipeline — referenced pipeline not statically verified.

ES-IP-004.title: Foreach step missing inner processor
ES-IP-004.description: A foreach processor has no inner processor object defined.
ES-IP-004.suggestion: Add processor: { "set": { ... } } or another processor inside foreach.
ES-IP-004.studentExplanation: Like a loop that says “for each item do nothing”—define the action.
ES-IP-004.voiceSummary: foreach missing inner processor. Define processor field.
ES-IP-004.technicalMessage: ES-IP-004: foreach processor missing non-empty processor child.

ES-IP-005.title: Script language not spelled out
ES-IP-005.description: The script processor omits lang; servers assume Painless anyway.
ES-IP-005.suggestion: Write lang explicitly so operators know which engine runs.
ES-IP-005.studentExplanation: Like shipping a battery without saying rechargeable—state it clearly.
ES-IP-005.voiceSummary: Ingest script processor missing lang. Set painless explicitly.
ES-IP-005.technicalMessage: ES-IP-005: script processor missing or blank lang field.

ES-IP-006.title: Grok pattern very complex
ES-IP-006.description: Many capture groups make grok expensive at ingest time.
ES-IP-006.suggestion: Simplify the pattern or split parsing across multiple processors.
ES-IP-006.studentExplanation: Like asking one regex to read a whole newspaper—give it smaller paragraphs.
ES-IP-006.voiceSummary: Grok pattern has many captures. Simplify.

ES-IP-007.title: Rename after remove on same field
ES-IP-007.description: A remove step drops a field that a later rename still references.
ES-IP-007.suggestion: Reorder processors so rename happens before remove, or rename a field that still exists.
ES-IP-007.studentExplanation: Like erasing a name tag then asking someone to read it—fix the order.
ES-IP-007.voiceSummary: rename targets field already removed earlier. Reorder pipeline.
ES-IP-007.technicalMessage: ES-IP-007: rename field in removedFields set from prior remove processor.

ES-RX-001.title: Reindex runs single-threaded
ES-RX-001.description: Without slices, large reindexes bottleneck on one coordinator path.
ES-RX-001.suggestion: Set slices to auto or a positive integer to parallelize across shards.
ES-RX-001.studentExplanation: Like moving a warehouse with one hand truck—rent more carts.
ES-RX-001.voiceSummary: Reindex without slices. Add parallel slices.
ES-RX-001.technicalMessage: ES-RX-001: reindex body missing slices field.

ES-RX-002.title: Reindex script ignores delete events
ES-RX-002.description: The script reads ctx._source but never checks ctx.op for deletes or no-ops.
ES-RX-002.studentExplanation: Like packing boxes without checking if the order was cancelled.
ES-RX-002.voiceSummary: Reindex script uses ctx._source without ctx.op guard.

ES-RX-003.title: Same-cluster reindex note
ES-RX-003.description: Reindex without a remote block copies within the cluster, typical for migrations.
ES-RX-003.suggestion: For cross-cluster copies add source.remote with the remote cluster settings.
ES-RX-003.studentExplanation: Like photocopying in-house versus mailing to another office—this path is in-house.
ES-RX-003.voiceSummary: Same-cluster reindex pattern. Add remote for cross-cluster.
ES-RX-003.technicalMessage: ES-RX-003: reindex source object without remote field (INFO advisory).

ES-ILM-001.title: Hot phase without rollover
ES-ILM-001.description: The hot phase never rolls indices, so they can grow without bound.
ES-ILM-001.suggestion: Add rollover with max age, size, or document count thresholds.
ES-ILM-001.studentExplanation: Like a trash can with no liner change schedule—it overflows eventually.
ES-ILM-001.voiceSummary: ILM hot phase missing rollover action.
ES-ILM-001.technicalMessage: ES-ILM-001: hot phase actions missing rollover object.

ES-ILM-002.title: Delete phase without retention delay
ES-ILM-002.description: The delete phase lacks min_age, so timing of deletes is unclear or immediate.
ES-ILM-002.suggestion: Set min_age such as 90d so deletion happens after a clear retention window.
ES-ILM-002.studentExplanation: Like tossing files with no “keep for one year” rule—define how long to wait.
ES-ILM-002.voiceSummary: ILM delete phase missing min_age. Set retention window.
ES-ILM-002.technicalMessage: ES-ILM-002: delete phase missing or blank min_age.

ES-ILM-003.title: Shrink listed before force merge
ES-ILM-003.description: Warm phase lists shrink before forcemerge, which is usually the opposite order.
ES-ILM-003.suggestion: Put forcemerge before shrink for clearer maintenance flow.
ES-ILM-003.studentExplanation: Like folding laundry before washing—swap to the usual efficient order.
ES-ILM-003.voiceSummary: ILM warm: shrink before forcemerge. Reorder actions.
ES-ILM-003.technicalMessage: ES-ILM-003: shrinkPos < fmPos in warm phase actions object field order.

ES-ILM-004.title: Cold phase without freeze
ES-ILM-004.description: Cold storage keeps indices heavier in memory than a frozen index would.
ES-ILM-004.suggestion: Add freeze in cold when you want read-only archival with lower heap use.
ES-ILM-004.studentExplanation: Like storing winter coats unpacked—freezing packs them tighter.
ES-ILM-004.voiceSummary: Cold phase missing freeze action. Consider freeze.
ES-ILM-004.technicalMessage: ES-ILM-004: cold phase actions object without freeze action.

ES-ILM-005.title: No delete phase in lifecycle policy
ES-ILM-005.description: Without delete, old indices may never leave the cluster automatically.
ES-ILM-005.suggestion: Add a delete phase or another managed cleanup path for aged data.
ES-ILM-005.studentExplanation: Like a closet that never gets cleaned—eventually there is no room.
ES-ILM-005.voiceSummary: ILM policy has no delete phase. Add cleanup phase.
ES-ILM-005.technicalMessage: ES-ILM-005: phases object missing delete phase key.

ES-SNP-001.title: Snapshot repository not compressed
ES-SNP-001.description: The repository settings leave compression off, so snapshots use more disk.
ES-SNP-001.suggestion: Enable compress where the repository type supports it.
ES-SNP-001.studentExplanation: Like mailing clothes without vacuum bags—they take more space.
ES-SNP-001.voiceSummary: Snapshot repo compress false. Enable if supported.

ES-SNP-002.title: Restore without rename pattern
ES-SNP-002.description: Restoring without rename can overwrite existing index names.
ES-SNP-002.suggestion: Set rename_pattern and rename_replacement to land on new names first.
ES-SNP-002.studentExplanation: Like moving furniture into a room that still has furniture—label new rooms.
ES-SNP-002.voiceSummary: Restore missing rename_pattern. Map to new index names.
ES-SNP-002.technicalMessage: ES-SNP-002: snapshot restore body without rename_pattern text.

ES-SNP-003.title: Repository lacks verification hint
ES-SNP-003.description: No schedule or inactivity verify hint is present for the repository.
ES-SNP-003.suggestion: Periodically verify the repository or use SLM with a cron schedule.
ES-SNP-003.studentExplanation: Like a smoke detector with no test reminder—schedule a check.
ES-SNP-003.voiceSummary: Repository missing verify schedule hint. Plan verification.
ES-SNP-003.technicalMessage: ES-SNP-003: no verify_after_inactivity or schedule on repository definition.

ES-SNP-004.title: Snapshot includes global cluster state
ES-SNP-004.description: include_global_state true snapshots cluster metadata, which can surprise production restores.
ES-SNP-004.suggestion: Prefer false unless you intentionally restore global metadata.
ES-SNP-004.studentExplanation: Like backing up the whole building blueprint when you only meant one apartment.
ES-SNP-004.voiceSummary: Snapshot include_global_state true. Prefer false unless intended.
ES-SNP-004.technicalMessage: ES-SNP-004: snapshot create include_global_state boolean true.

ES-TPL-001.title: Template params incomplete
ES-TPL-001.description: The Mustache template references names that are missing from the params object.
ES-TPL-001.suggestion: Add each name under params or add default inverted sections in the template.
ES-TPL-001.studentExplanation: Like a form missing blanks the letter asks for—fill every referenced field.
ES-TPL-001.voiceSummary: Mustache params missing keys. Add them.
ES-TPL-001.technicalMessage: ES-TPL-001: mustacheInterpolationRoots not subset of params keys.

ES-TPL-002.title: Optional params without defaults
ES-TPL-002.description: Sections use parameters that lack default inverted blocks in the template.
ES-TPL-002.suggestion: Add {{^name}}...{{/name}} for optional parameters that may be absent.
ES-TPL-002.studentExplanation: Like optional toppings without a “none” line on the order form.

ES-TPL-003.title: Template looks like unsafe string assembly
ES-TPL-003.description: The template text resembles patterns used in injection or unsafe dynamic queries.
ES-TPL-003.suggestion: Build queries from structured JSON fields instead of pasting user text into clauses.
ES-TPL-003.studentExplanation: Like gluing stranger sentences into a contract—use placeholders and structured fields.
ES-TPL-003.voiceSummary: Mustache template matches SQL-injection-like heuristics. Refactor.
ES-TPL-003.technicalMessage: ES-TPL-003: containsSqlInjectionLikePattern(src) true on mustache template.

ES-TPL-004.title: Validate template before production
ES-TPL-004.description: Templates by id or with interpolations should be dry-run with real parameters.
ES-TPL-004.suggestion: Call POST _render/template with production-like params before go-live.
ES-TPL-004.studentExplanation: Like rehearsing a speech with the actual microphone, not only on paper.
ES-TPL-004.voiceSummary: Use render template API to validate mustache with params.
ES-TPL-004.technicalMessage: ES-TPL-004: stored template / id-only / interpolation validation advisory (ElasticsearchAnalyzer).

ES-RND-001.title: Render params line up cleanly
ES-RND-001.description: Every referenced template parameter is supplied and there are no unused keys.
ES-RND-001.suggestion: Keep using this dry-run style for CI checks on stored templates too.
ES-RND-001.studentExplanation: Like a dress rehearsal where every actor showed up with the right props.
ES-RND-001.voiceSummary: Render template params complete and aligned. Good practice.
ES-RND-001.technicalMessage: ES-RND-001: emitRenderTemplateParamsCompleteInfo — params cover template roots, no unused.

ES-RND-002.title: Unused render parameter keys
ES-RND-002.description: The params object includes keys the template never references.
ES-RND-002.suggestion: Remove unused keys to keep requests obvious and avoid confusion.
ES-RND-002.studentExplanation: Like packing shoes for a beach trip you never mention—drop unused baggage.
ES-RND-002.voiceSummary: Render params contain unused keys. Remove extras.
ES-RND-002.technicalMessage: ES-RND-002: provided param keys not in mustacheInterpolationRoots.

CQL-BATCH-APPLY.title: Batch not closed properly
CQL-BATCH-APPLY.description: A Cassandra batch block does not end with the required closing phrase.
CQL-BATCH-APPLY.suggestion: Finish the batch with APPLY BATCH before the semicolon so the server accepts it.
CQL-BATCH-APPLY.studentExplanation: Like sealing a letter but forgetting to write “end of letter” on a form that requires it.
CQL-BATCH-APPLY.voiceSummary: BATCH must end with APPLY BATCH.

CQL-BATCH-LARGE.title: Very large Cassandra batch
CQL-BATCH-LARGE.description: The batch contains a large number of statements, which is a known anti-pattern.
CQL-BATCH-LARGE.suggestion: Split into smaller batches or use asynchronous writes with a safe concurrency limit.
CQL-BATCH-LARGE.studentExplanation: Like loading one elevator with everyone in the building—several trips are safer.
CQL-BATCH-LARGE.voiceSummary: Large CQL batch warning. Split or go async.
CQL-BATCH-LARGE.technicalMessage: CQL-BATCH-LARGE: estimated batch count > 100 && <= CAS-BATCH-001 threshold branch.

DDB-MGMT-JSON.title: Invalid JSON for DynamoDB management
DDB-MGMT-JSON.description: Streams, backups, or PITR payloads must be valid JSON objects.
DDB-MGMT-JSON.suggestion: Fix braces, commas, and quotes, then resend the management-style payload.
DDB-MGMT-JSON.studentExplanation: Like a customs form smudged unreadable—the officer sends it back unread.

DDB-STR-VIEW.title: Unknown stream view type
DDB-STR-VIEW.description: StreamViewType is not one of the values DynamoDB recognizes.
DDB-STR-VIEW.suggestion: Use KEYS_ONLY, NEW_IMAGE, OLD_IMAGE, or NEW_AND_OLD_IMAGES.
DDB-STR-VIEW.studentExplanation: Like picking a camera mode that your phone model does not offer.
DDB-STR-VIEW.technicalMessage: DDB-STR-VIEW: StreamViewType not in STREAM_VIEW_TYPES set.

DDB-TX-JSON.title: Invalid JSON for a transaction request
DDB-TX-JSON.description: TransactItems must live inside a single well-formed JSON object.
DDB-TX-JSON.suggestion: Validate JSON structure and ensure TransactItems is an array before retrying.
DDB-TX-JSON.studentExplanation: Like a shopping list torn in half—the cashier cannot read the cart.

ES-CROSS-003.title: Mixed Elasticsearch API styles in one script
ES-CROSS-003.description: The script combines search bodies with ingest, reindex, ILM, snapshots, or templates.
ES-CROSS-003.suggestion: Send each kind to its proper HTTP path (/_search, /_ingest/pipeline, /_reindex, etc.).
ES-CROSS-003.studentExplanation: Like mailing letters, parcels, and legal filings in one envelope—sort them by counter.
ES-CROSS-003.voiceSummary: Script mixes Elasticsearch API payload kinds. Split requests.

ES-CROSS-004.title: Several ingest pipeline definitions together
ES-CROSS-004.description: Multiple pipeline PUT definitions appear in one script; the cluster does not auto-chain them.
ES-CROSS-004.suggestion: Document dependencies between pipelines in your runbook or CI.
ES-CROSS-004.studentExplanation: Like stacking several recipe cards—order of filing matters only to your kitchen, not the oven.
ES-CROSS-004.voiceSummary: Multiple ingest pipeline blocks in one script. Document order.

LUA-PARSE-ERROR.title: Lua script could not be parsed
LUA-PARSE-ERROR.description: The Redis Lua snippet has a syntax or token problem the analyzer cannot read.
LUA-PARSE-ERROR.suggestion: Check quotes, brackets, if/for/end structure, and comments near the reported line.
LUA-PARSE-ERROR.studentExplanation: Like a knitting pattern with a skipped row—the next stitches do not line up.
LUA-PARSE-ERROR.voiceSummary: Lua parse or lex error in script.
LUA-PARSE-ERROR.technicalMessage: LUA-PARSE-ERROR: LuaLexer.LexException or LuaParser.ParseException in LuaAnalyzer.

MGO-AGG-PERF-001.title: Long pipeline without an early filter
MGO-AGG-PERF-001.description: Many aggregation stages run before a selective match, so the server may scan too many documents.
MGO-AGG-PERF-001.suggestion: Put a selective $match stage first to shrink the working set.
MGO-AGG-PERF-001.studentExplanation: Like washing every dish in the building before choosing which shelf to fill—rinse the pile first.
MGO-AGG-PERF-001.voiceSummary: Pipeline over five stages without leading $match. Add early $match.
MGO-AGG-PERF-001.technicalMessage: MGO-AGG-PERF-001: stageOpens.size()>5 && first stage != $match in scanAggregationRules.

MGO-ELEM-001.title: Element query with no real condition
MGO-ELEM-001.description: $elemMatch is present but empty, so it does not describe which array elements you want.
MGO-ELEM-001.suggestion: Add at least one predicate inside $elemMatch, for example a comparison on a field.
MGO-ELEM-001.studentExplanation: Like saying “any row in the spreadsheet” without saying which column to check.

MGO-GEO-IDX-001.title: Geospatial query needs an index
MGO-GEO-IDX-001.description: Near or within-style geo filters expect a 2dsphere or 2d index on the coordinate field.
MGO-GEO-IDX-001.suggestion: Create the right geo index before relying on these queries in production.
MGO-GEO-IDX-001.studentExplanation: Like using a map app offline with no downloaded tiles—the route cannot load.

MGO-GEO-NEAR-001.title: Near query without a distance cap
MGO-GEO-NEAR-001.description: A $near-style query has no $maxDistance, so results and cost can sprawl.
MGO-GEO-NEAR-001.suggestion: Add $maxDistance (and optionally $minDistance) to bound the search radius.
MGO-GEO-NEAR-001.studentExplanation: Like asking for every restaurant on Earth sorted by distance—say how far you will travel.

MGO-GEO-POLY-001.title: Polygon ring not closed
MGO-GEO-POLY-001.description: GeoJSON polygons must repeat the first coordinate as the last point of each ring.
MGO-GEO-POLY-001.suggestion: Close each linear ring by copying the first coordinate to the end.
MGO-GEO-POLY-001.studentExplanation: Like drawing a fence line that does not reconnect to the first post.
MGO-GEO-POLY-001.voiceSummary: GeoJSON polygon must close first and last point.

MGO-JS-101.title: Server script reads fields with “this”
MGO-JS-101.description: The snippet uses this to read document fields on the server, which is hard to audit and costly.
MGO-JS-101.suggestion: Prefer query operators, aggregation, or $expr instead of server-side JavaScript.
MGO-JS-101.studentExplanation: Like using a magic notebook that rewrites itself—harder for others to review safely.
MGO-JS-101.voiceSummary: Mongo server JS uses this. Prefer operators or $expr.
MGO-JS-101.technicalMessage: MGO-JS-101: MongoJsParser WORD_THIS in stripped JS.

MGO-JS-102.title: Dangerous call in server-side JavaScript
MGO-JS-102.description: Patterns such as eval, require, or Function appear in MongoDB-side JavaScript.
MGO-JS-102.suggestion: Remove these calls; they can enable abuse or unexpected behavior.
MGO-JS-102.studentExplanation: Like handing a stranger a master remote for your house—revoke risky calls.
MGO-JS-102.voiceSummary: Dangerous JS call in Mongo snippet. Remove eval or similar.

MGO-OID-001.title: ObjectId compared as plain text
MGO-OID-001.description: Comparing _id to a 24-character hex string without wrapping ObjectId will never match BSON ObjectIds.
MGO-OID-001.suggestion: Use ObjectId("...") or the correct BSON type your driver expects.
MGO-OID-001.studentExplanation: Like comparing a locker number written on paper to the metal chip inside the key—different shapes.
MGO-OID-001.technicalMessage: MGO-OID-001: scanObjectIdStringComparisons string hex next to _id field.

MGO-PUSH-001.title: Push several values without $each
MGO-PUSH-001.description: Pushing multiple array elements at once should use the $each helper for clarity and efficiency.
MGO-PUSH-001.studentExplanation: Like loading several plates on one tray tag—stack them using the tray’s divider.
MGO-PUSH-001.technicalMessage: MGO-PUSH-001: validatePushStyleArrays multi elements without $each modifier.

MGO-PULL-001.title: Pull rule is empty
MGO-PULL-001.description: An empty $pull filter can remove every matching element from an array unintentionally.
MGO-PULL-001.suggestion: Add explicit predicates to $pull or confirm you truly want to clear matching entries.
MGO-PULL-001.studentExplanation: Like “remove every sticker” with no rule about which sticker—everything may go.
MGO-PULL-001.voiceSummary: Empty $pull condition is risky. Add predicates.

MGO-TEXT-LANG-001.title: Text search language not recognized
MGO-TEXT-LANG-001.description: The $language value is not in MongoDB’s supported text language list.
MGO-TEXT-LANG-001.suggestion: Pick a supported language code or omit $language to use the index default.
MGO-TEXT-LANG-001.studentExplanation: Like choosing spell-check for a language your dictionary does not ship with.
MGO-TEXT-LANG-001.technicalMessage: MGO-TEXT-LANG-001: validateTextLanguage lang not in KNOWN_TEXT_LANGUAGES.

PNL-PARSE-ERROR.title: Painless script could not be parsed
PNL-PARSE-ERROR.description: The Elasticsearch Painless snippet has a syntax issue near the reported position.
PNL-PARSE-ERROR.suggestion: Check braces, semicolons, def usage, and comments around the error location.
PNL-PARSE-ERROR.studentExplanation: Like a formula in a spreadsheet with one extra parenthesis—the sheet stops calculating.

RDS-TX-005.title: Transaction left open at end of script
RDS-TX-005.description: A MULTI block never receives EXEC or DISCARD before the script ends.
RDS-TX-005.suggestion: Always close MULTI with EXEC to apply or DISCARD to cancel before finishing the script.
RDS-TX-005.studentExplanation: Like leaving a checkout lane mid-ring without paying or voiding—the register stays stuck.
RDS-TX-005.voiceSummary: Script ends inside MULTI. Send EXEC or DISCARD.
RDS-TX-005.technicalMessage: RDS-TX-005: inMulti true at end of analyzeCrossScriptOnly / RedisAnalyzer cross-script.

SELECT.purpose: Retrieves columns from one or more tables.
SELECT.example: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = true

FROM.purpose: Specifies which table(s) data comes from.
FROM.example: SELECT * FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id

WHERE.purpose: Filters rows that match a condition.
WHERE.example: SELECT * FROM users WHERE age >= 18 AND country = 'US'

DELETE.example: DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at < NOW()

DELETE_FROM.purpose: Deletes rows from the named table.

UPDATE.purpose: Changes column values in existing rows.
UPDATE.syntax: UPDATE table SET col = value WHERE condition
UPDATE.example: UPDATE users SET active = false WHERE last_login < '2025-01-01'

JOIN.syntax: FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id
JOIN.example: SELECT u.name, o.total FROM users u JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id

LIMIT.purpose: Caps how many rows the query returns.
LIMIT.example: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 20

keywords.LEFT_JOIN: Combines tables keeping all rows from the left
keywords.RIGHT_JOIN: Combines tables keeping all rows from the right
keywords.INNER_JOIN: Combines only matching rows from both tables
keywords.FULL_JOIN: Combines all rows from both tables
keywords.INSERT_INTO: Inserts new rows into a table
keywords.FROM: Specifies the table to read data from
keywords.JOIN: Combines rows from two or more tables
keywords.HAVING: Filters groups (like WHERE but for GROUP BY)
keywords.LIMIT: Limits the number of rows returned
keywords.OFFSET: Skips the first rows of the result
keywords.INSERT: Inserts new rows into a table
keywords.CREATE: Creates a new table or object
keywords.DROP: Permanently removes a table or object
keywords.EXCEPT: Returns rows from the first query not in the second
keywords.INTERSECT: Returns rows common to both queries

OPT-001.what: You are selecting ALL columns from the table
OPT-001.why: Like ordering a whole book when you only need one chapter — the database does extra work
OPT-001.correctedExample: SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE active = true

OPT-002.what: This query may not use any index
OPT-002.why: Without an index, the database checks every row one by one, like searching a book without an index at the back

OPT-003.what: This pattern may run an extra query per result
OPT-003.why: With 100 users you might run 101 queries instead of 1 — very slow
OPT-003.correctedExample: Use JOIN to fetch everything in a single query

risks.concat: String concatenation in the query: high SQL injection risk.
risks.dynamicExec: Dynamic execution detected: the query may be altered at runtime.
risks.mongoWhere: MongoDB $where usage: may execute server-side JavaScript.
risks.redisEval: Redis EVAL with embedded script: command injection risk.
risks.esScript: Inline Elasticsearch scripts: review permissions and input validation.

fixes.concat: Use prepared parameters (? or :name) instead of joining strings with +.
fixes.dynamicExec: Avoid dynamic EXEC; use parameterized queries or fixed procedures.
fixes.drop: Confirm environment, back up data, and restrict permissions before DROP.
fixes.mongoWhere: Avoid $where; use safe query operators ($eq, $in, etc.).
fixes.redisEval: Prefer parameterized commands or Lua with ARGV instead of concatenating input.
fixes.esScript: Restrict scripts, validate inputs, and use reviewed templates.
